name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ٱرْجِعُونِ (٩٩)
```

```english
When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back, (99)
```

```arabic
لَعَلِّىٓ أَعْمَلُ صَـٰلِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ ۚ كَلَّآ ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَآئِلُهَا ۖ وَمِن وَرَآئِهِم بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
that I may act righteously in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says.’ And before them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected. (100)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَلَآ أَنسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَلَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (١٠١)
```

```english
When the Trumpet is blown, there will be no ties between them on that day, nor will they ask [about] each other. (101)
```

```arabic
فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٠٢)
```

```english
Then those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales—it is they who are the felicitous. (102)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فِى جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٠٣)
```

```english
As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales—they will be the ones who have ruined their souls, and they will remain in hell [forever]. (103)
```

```arabic
تَلْفَحُ وُجُوهَهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَهُمْ فِيهَا كَـٰلِحُونَ (١٠٤)
```

```english
The Fire will scorch their faces, while they snarl baring their teeth. (104)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
‘Was it not that My signs were recited to you but you would deny them?’ (105)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنَا شِقْوَتُنَا وَكُنَّا قَوْمًا ضَآلِّينَ (١٠٦)
```

```english
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Our wretchedness overcame us, and we were an astray lot. (106)
```

```arabic
رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْهَا فَإِنْ عُدْنَا فَإِنَّا ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٠٧)
```

```english
Our Lord! Bring us out of this! Then, if we revert [to our previous conduct], we will indeed be wrongdoers.’ (107)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱخْسَـُٔوا۟ فِيهَا وَلَا تُكَلِّمُونِ (١٠٨)
```

```english
He will say, ‘Begone in it, and do not speak to Me! (108)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِى يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (١٠٩)
```

```english
Indeed there was a part of My servants who would say, ‘‘Our Lord! We have believed. So forgive us, and have mercy on us, and You are the best of the merciful.’’ (109)
```

```arabic
فَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّىٰٓ أَنسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِى وَكُنتُم مِّنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ (١١٠)
```

```english
But you took them by ridicule until they made you forget My remembrance, and you used to laugh at them. (110)
```

```arabic
إِنِّى جَزَيْتُهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (١١١)
```

```english
Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience. They are indeed the triumphant.’ (111)
```

```arabic
قَـٰلَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ (١١٢)
```

```english
He will say, ‘How many years did you remain on earth?’ (112)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْعَآدِّينَ (١١٣)
```

```english
They will say, ‘We remained for a day, or part of a day; yet ask those who keep the count.’ (113)
```

```arabic
قَـٰلَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ۖ لَّوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١١٤)
```

```english
He will say, ‘You only remained a little; if only you had known. (114)
```

```arabic
أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ عَبَثًا وَأَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا تُرْجَعُونَ (١١٥)
```

```english
Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly, and that you will not be brought back to Us?’ (115)
```

```arabic
فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْكَرِيمِ (١١٦)
```

```english
So exalted is Allah, the True Sovereign, there is no god except Him, the Lord of the Noble Throne. (116)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ لَا بُرْهَـٰنَ لَهُۥ بِهِۦ فَإِنَّمَا حِسَابُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١١٧)
```

```english
Whoever invokes besides Allah another god of which he has no proof, his reckoning will indeed rest with his Lord. Indeed the faithless will not be felicitous. (117)
```

```arabic
وَقُل رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ وَٱرْحَمْ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (١١٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘My Lord, forgive and have mercy, and You are the best of the merciful.’ (118)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses detail the discourse regarding the punishment of the Hereafter (الآخرة) — *the final and eternal abode after resurrection* — with which Allah had threatened them in the course of the preceding verses. This punishment spans from the day of death until the Day of Resurrection (يوم البعث), and then unto eternity. The verses mention that the life of this world (الحياة الدنيا), which deceived them and diverted them from the Hereafter, is but a trifle, if only they knew. The Surah then concludes with His command to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ask Him for that which He narrated from His believing servants who are the successful ones in the Hereafter: "My Lord! Forgive and have mercy, and You are the best of those who are merciful." The Surah had opened with the declaration that they are the felicitous ones (مفلحون) and the inheritors of Paradise (الجنة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ** — **When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back (99)**. The word "until" (حَتَّى) is connected to the preceding description of their attributing to Allah that which He is transcendent (منزه) above, and their associating partners (شركهم) with Him. The intervening verses are a parenthetical insertion (اعتراض) in the speech; meaning: they continue to associate partners with Him and describe Him with that which He is transcendent above, while being deceived by the wealth and children with which We provide them, until death comes to one of them.

Regarding His saying: **قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ** (he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back’), it appears that the address is to the angels (الملائكة) tasked with seizing his soul. The word "My Lord" (رَبِّ) is a parenthetical plea for help (استغاثة) with the vocative particle omitted. The meaning is: he says—while crying out to his Lord—"Take me back."

It has also been said that the address is to the Lord, the Exalted, and the plural form is for glorification (التعظيم), similar to the saying of Pharaoh's wife to him, as narrated by Allah: **قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِي وَلَكَ لا تَقْتُلُوهُ** (A comfort to the eye for me and you. Do not kill him).

It has further been said that this is a case of pluralizing the verb to signify the repetition of the request. The meaning would be: "My Lord, take me back, take me back, take me back," as has been said regarding the poetic verse:

*Stay (plural), let us weep at the memory of a beloved and an abode*
*At the edge of the shifting sands between al-Dakhul and Hawmal*

Meaning: "Stay, stay, let us weep."

The critique of these two views is that the use of the plural for glorification (التعظيم), even if its existence in the Arabic language were proven, is rare (شاذ) and the speech of the Exalted should not be interpreted based upon it. Even rarer is the pluralization of the verb with the meaning mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ كَلَّا إِنَّها كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قائِلُها** — **that I may do righteous deeds in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says (100)**. The word "perhaps" (لعل) expresses hope (الترجي); it is a hope they cling to upon witnessing the punishment looming over them. They often mention returning with a promise of righteous action, as in their saying: **فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً** — **so send us back, that we may act righteously** (32:12). Sometimes they mention it in the form of a wish (التمني), as in their saying: **يا لَيْتَنا نُرَدُّ وَلا نُكَذِّبَ بِآياتِ رَبِّنا** — **‘Would that we were sent back, and then we would not deny the signs of our Lord** (6:27).

His saying: **أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ** (that I may do righteous deeds in what I have left behind) means: that I may perform righteous deeds regarding the wealth I left behind by spending it in piety and charity, and everything that contains the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified.

It has been said that "what I left behind" refers to the world (الدنيا) which he left at death, and "righteous deeds" (العمل الصالح) is more general than financial worship, including prayer (الصلاة), fasting (الصوم), pilgrimage (الحج), and the like. This is a fine interpretation, though the first is more apparent.

His saying: **كَلَّا إِنَّها كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قائِلُها** (By no means! These are mere words that he says) means: he shall not return to the world. This statement—"Take me back that I may do righteous deeds in what I have left behind"—is merely a word he utters; it has no effect other than being a word he says. This is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that his request will not be granted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** — **and ahead of them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected (100)**. The intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — is the barrier (الحاجز) between two things, as in His saying: **بَيْنَهُما بَرْزَخٌ لا يَبْغِيانِ** — **between them is a barrier which they do not overstep** (55:20). The meaning of it being "behind them" (وَرائِهِمْ) is that it is in front of them, encompassing them. It is called "behind them" with the consideration that it pursues them, just as the future time is in front of a person, yet it is said: "Such a day is behind you," meaning that time pursues the person to pass over him. This is the meaning of the saying of some that "behind" (وراء) carries the meaning of "encompassing" (الإحاطة). The Exalted said: **وَكانَ وَراءَهُمْ مَلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْباً** — **and there was a king ahead of them who seized every ship by force** (18:79).

What is meant by this intermediate realm (البرزخ) is the world of the grave (عالم القبر), which is the imaginal world (عالم المثال) in which a human being lives after death until the standing of the Hour (قيام الساعة), as indicated by the context and other verses, and as supported by numerous narrations (روايات) from the paths of the Shi'a from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, as well as from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah. This was previously discussed in the first volume of this book.

It has been said that the verse means there is a barrier between them and the world that prevents them from returning to it until the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). Since it is known that there is no return after the Resurrection, this serves as an emphasis on their non-return and their total despair of ever returning to the world.

The critique of this is that the apparent context indicates the establishment of the barrier between the world and the day they are resurrected, not between them and the return to the world. If the meaning were that death is a barrier between them and returning to the world, the restriction by His saying "until the day they will be resurrected" (إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ) would be redundant. This is not because it would imply by way of its counter-implication (المفهوم) that they return to the world after the Resurrection—for there is no return after the Resurrection—but rather because the restriction itself would be pointless, even if we assume they knew from external sources or previous verses that there is no return after the Resurrection.

Furthermore, their claim that it is an emphasis on non-return by making them despair of returning absolutely, while also saying that the non-return after the Resurrection is known externally, is somewhat contradictory. Rather, the meaning would revert to emphasizing the absolute negation of return—understood from "By no means!" (كَلَّا)—with the negation of a temporary, limited return by His saying "until the day they will be resurrected." Understand this well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَلا يَتَساءَلُونَ** — **When the Trumpet is blown, there will be no ties of kinship between them on that day, nor will they ask of one another (101)**. What is meant here is the second blast (النفخة الثانية) in which the dead are brought to life, not the first blast in which the living die, as some have said. This is because the consequences mentioned—the negation of kinship, the lack of questioning, and the heaviness or lightness of the scales (الميزان)—are all effects of the second blast.

His saying: **فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ** (there will be no ties of kinship between them) is a negation of the effects of kinship by negating its very basis. That which necessitates the preservation and consideration of kinship (الأنساب) are the worldly needs that drive a human toward social life, which is built upon the formation of the household. The domestic society results in mutual recognition, affection, and various forms of cooperation and support, and all other means by which worldly life continues. The Day of Resurrection is the time for the recompense of deeds (جزاء الأعمال) and the falling away of causes (الأسباب), among which are deeds. Thus, there is no place in it for worldly causes, including kinship with its requirements, properties, and effects.

His saying: **وَلا يَتَساءَلُونَ** (nor will they ask of one another) mentions the most apparent effect of kinship, which is the questioning between relatives about one another's state, for the purpose of aiding or seeking aid in needs, to bring benefits or repel harms.

This verse does not contradict what occurs in other places, such as His saying: **وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ** — **And they will turn to one another, questioning each other** (37:27). That is a narration of the questioning of the people of Paradise after entering it, and the questioning of the people of Hell after entering it. This verse, however, negates questioning during the time of reckoning (الحساب) and judgment (القضاء).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** — **Then those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales—it is they who are the felicitous (102)**, until the end of the two verses. The "scales" (الموازين) is the plural of "scale" (الميزان) or the plural of "that which is weighed" (الموزون), which is the deed (العمل) that is weighed on that day. The discussion regarding the meaning of the scale and its heaviness or lightness was previously covered in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah al-A'raf.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَلْفَحُ وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ وَهُمْ فِيها كالِحُونَ** — **The Fire will scorch their faces, and they will be grim in it (104)**. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "Scorching (اللفح) and blowing (النفح) have the same meaning, except that scorching is more severe in its effect and greater than blowing. It is a type of hot wind (السموم) hitting the face, while blowing is the wind hitting the face. Grimness (الكلوح) is the contraction of the lips away from the teeth until the teeth are exposed." End quote.

The meaning is: the flame of the Fire will strike their faces until their lips contract and reveal their teeth, like roasted heads.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ تَكُنْ آياتِي تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ** — **‘Were not My signs recited to you... (105)** etc. That is, it will be said to them: Were not My signs recited to you, and you used to deny them?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا رَبَّنا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنا شِقْوَتُنا وَكُنَّا قَوْماً ضالِّينَ** — **They will say, ‘Our Lord! Our wretchedness overcame us, and we were an erring people (106)**. Wretchedness (الشقوة), misery (الشقاوة), and distress (الشقاء) are the opposite of felicity (السعادة). The felicity of a thing is the good (الخير) specific to it, and its wretchedness is the loss of that, or if you wish, say: the evil (الشر) specific to it.

Their saying: **غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنا شِقْوَتُنا** (Our wretchedness overcame us) means: our wretchedness conquered and dominated us. In attributing wretchedness to themselves, there is an allusion that they had a hand in their wretchedness through their acquisition (اكتساب) of it by their own evil choice (سوء اختيارهم). The evidence for this is their subsequent saying: **رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ** — **Our Lord! Bring us out of it; then if we return [to rebellion], we shall indeed be wrongdoers** (107). This is a promise from them to perform good deeds; if these deeds were not connected to their voluntary acquisition, the promise would be meaningless, as their state after being brought out would be the same as before.

They considered themselves overcome by wretchedness, having taken their souls as neutral in their essences, capable of receiving both felicity and wretchedness, but wretchedness overcame and occupied the place. This wretchedness was the wretchedness of their own souls—that is, a wretchedness necessitated by their evil choice and their evil deeds—because they assumed their souls were devoid of inherent felicity or wretchedness. Thus, the attribution of wretchedness to their souls and its connection to them is only from the perspective of their evil choice and evil deeds.

In short, it is an admission from them of the completion of the proof (تمام الحجة) and the attachment of wretchedness, as evidenced by the occurrence of the verse after His saying: "Were not My signs recited to you..." etc.

Then they followed their saying "Our wretchedness overcame us" with their saying "and we were an erring people" (وَكُنَّا قَوْماً ضالِّينَ) to emphasize their admission. They only admitted their sin to use it as a means to escape the punishment and return to the world to acquire felicity. They had observed in the world that the admission of a rebellious sinner of his sin and wrongdoing constitutes a repentance (توبة) that purifies him and saves him from the consequences of the sin. They know that this day is a day of recompense, not a day of action, and repentance and admission of sin are among the actions. However, this is of the nature of the manifestation of ingrained dispositions (الملكات), just as they lie on that day and deny things despite the manifestation of the truth and its witnessing, because the disposition of lying and denial is established in their souls. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُ كَما يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ** — **On the day when Allah will resurrect them all, they will swear to Him as they swear to you** (58:18), and He said: **ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ قالُوا ضَلُّوا عَنَّا بَلْ لَمْ نَكُنْ نَدْعُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ شَيْئاً** — **Then they will be asked, ‘Where are those you used to associate [with Allah] besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us. Rather, we did not use to invoke anything before’** (40:73-74).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ** — **Our Lord! Bring us out of it; then if we return [to rebellion], we shall indeed be wrongdoers (107)**. This is a request from them to return to the world, as indicated by other verses. It is of the nature of seeking the effect by seeking its cause. Their intention is to perform righteous deeds after having repented through the aforementioned admission, thereby becoming among those who repented and performed righteous deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ اخْسَؤُا فِيها وَلا تُكَلِّمُونِ** — **He will say, ‘Begone into it, and do not speak to Me!’ (108)**. Al-Raghib said: "I rebuked the dog (خسأت الكلب) so it was driven away (فخسأ); that is, I scolded it with contempt so it was repelled. This is when you say to it: *ikhsa'* (begone)." End quote. Thus, in the speech, there is a metaphor by allusion (استعارة بالكناية), and the meaning is to rebuke them into distancing themselves and to cut off all speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْ عِبادِي يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا آمَنَّا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ** — **Indeed there was a part of My servants who used to say, ‘Our Lord! We have believed. So forgive us, and have mercy on us, and You are the best of those who are merciful’ (109)**. These are the believers in the world, and their faith was a repentance and a return to Allah, just as Allah called it repentance in His speech. Their request for the encompassing of mercy—which is certainly the specific mercy for the believers—was a request from them that He grant them success for felicity so they might perform righteous deeds and enter Paradise. They sought this through His name, "the best of those who are merciful."

Thus, what the believers said in the world meant repentance and asking for the attainment of felicity, and that is exactly what these people said, which meant repentance and asking for the attainment of felicity. The only difference between them is the station (الموقف).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّى أَنْسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِي وَكُنْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ** — **But you took them for a laughingstock until they made you forget My remembrance, and you used to laugh at them (110)**. The pronouns of address refer to the disbelievers, and the third-person pronouns refer to the believers. The context bears witness that what is meant by "My remembrance" (ذِكْرِي) is the saying of the believers: "Our Lord! We have believed. So forgive us, and have mercy on us..." etc., which is the same as the meaning of what the disbelievers say in the Fire.

His saying: **حَتَّى أَنْسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِي** (until they made you forget My remembrance) means: your preoccupation with mocking the believers and laughing at them made you forget My remembrance. In attributing the causing of forgetfulness to the believers rather than to the mockery itself, there is an indication that the believers held no status in their eyes except as objects of mockery.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنِّي جَزَيْتُهُمُ الْيَوْمَ بِما صبروا أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ** — **Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience; they are indeed the triumphant (111)**. What is meant by "today" is the Day of Recompense (يوم الجزاء). The object of their patience (الصبر) is known from the context and is omitted for brevity; meaning: they were patient in My remembrance despite your mockery of them for it. His saying: **أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ** (they are indeed the triumphant) is phrased to signify exclusivity (الحصر); meaning: they are the successful ones, not you.

These four verses—from "Begone into it" to "the triumphant"—constitute a definitive despair for the disbelievers regarding success through what they clung to of admitting sin and asking to return to the world. Their summary is: Despair of what you seek with this speech—the admission and the request—for it is an action that only benefited in the abode of action (دار العمل), which is the world. A group of My servants used to take it as a means to success, while you used to mock and laugh at them until you abandoned it and replaced it with mocking them. Now that it is today—the Day of Recompense, not the day of action—they have triumphed by the reward of what they did on the day of action, while you remain empty-handed, wanting to seek a means through action today, which is the Day of Recompense, not the day of action.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ** — **He will say, ‘How many years did you tarry on earth?’ (112)**. Among the things Allah will ask people about on the Day of Resurrection is the duration of their stay on earth. This is mentioned in several places in His speech. What is meant by it is the question regarding the duration of their stay in the graves (القبور), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ما لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ ساعَةٍ** — **On the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear that they had tarried but for an hour** (30:55), and His saying: **كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ** — **On the day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not tarried but for an hour of a day** (46:35), and other verses. Thus, there is no place for the saying of some that what is meant is the stay in the world, nor the possibility suggested by some that it is the sum of the stay in the world and the intermediate realm (البرزخ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَسْئَلِ الْعادِّينَ** — **They will say, ‘We tarried a day, or part of a day; so ask those who keep count’ (113)**. The apparent context is that what is meant by "day" is one of the days of the world. They deemed the stay on earth to be very short when they compared it to the eternal duration that appears to them on the Day of Resurrection and which they witness.

This is supported by what occurs in another place where they estimate it as an hour, and in another place as an evening or its morning.

His saying: **فَسْئَلِ الْعادِّينَ** (so ask those who keep count) means: we are not good at enumerating it, so ask those who count it. This has been interpreted as the angels who count the days, and this is not far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ إِن لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً لَوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — **He will say, ‘You tarried but a little, if you only knew (114)**. The speaker is Allah, the Glorified. In the speech is a confirmation of their deeming the stay in the graves to be short. It also serves as a preamble for what follows in His saying: "if you only knew," which contains a sense of wishing.

The meaning is: Allah said: The matter is as you said; you did not stay but a little. If only you had known in the world that you would not stay in your graves but a little and then be resurrected, so that you would not have denied the Resurrection and would not have been afflicted with this eternal punishment. The wishing (التمني) in the speech of the Exalted, like the expressing of hope (الترجي), refers back to the addressee or the situation.

Some have made "if" (لَوْ) in the verse conditional (شرطية), with the sentence being a condition whose result (الجزاء) is omitted, and they labored to justify the speech in a way that sound taste does not accept, and it is far from the context as is apparent. Even further is making "if" (لو) connective (وصلية), as the connective "if" does not come without the conjunction "and" (واو العطف).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّما خَلَقْناكُمْ عَبَثاً** — **Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly (115)**, until His saying: **رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْكريمِ** — **the Lord of the Noble Throne (116)**. After explaining what will face them of the states of death, then the stay in the intermediate realm, then the Resurrection with its reckoning and recompense, He rebuked them for their supposition that they would not be resurrected. For in that is a boldness against Allah by attributing aimlessness (العبث) to Him. Then He pointed to the proof against aimlessness.

So His saying: **أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ** (Did you suppose...) etc., means: if the matter is as We have informed you—of your regret upon witnessing death, then the stay in the graves, then the Resurrection, then the reckoning and recompense—do you then think that We created you aimlessly, living and dying without any lasting purpose in your creation, and that you would not return to Us?

His saying: **فَتَعالَى اللهُ الْمَلِكُ الْحَقُّ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْكَرِيمِ** — **So exalted be Allah, the True Sovereign, there is no god but He, the Lord of the Noble Throne (116)**, is an indication of a proof that establishes the Resurrection and refutes their denial, in the form of glorification (تنزيه). For He, the Exalted, described Himself in the word of glorification with four attributes: that He is Sovereign (ملك), that He is Truth (حق), that there is no god but He, and that He is the Lord of the Noble Throne.

Thus, He has the right to decree whatever He wills of beginning and return, life and death, and provision—His decree being effective and His command being executed due to His sovereignty (ملك). Whatever decree issues from Him can only be truth (حق), for He is Truth, and nothing issues from the Truth, insofar as it is truth, except truth, without it being aimless or vain. Then, since it might be imagined that there is another source of decree with Him that could decree what would invalidate His decree, He described Himself as "there is no god"—that is, no object of worship—"but He." The god (الإله) is worshipped due to his lordship (ربوبية); so if there is no god other than Him, then He is the Lord of the Throne—the Noble Throne of the world—which is the gathering point of the reins of all affairs, and from it issue the decrees and commands running within it.

In summary, He is the one from whom every decree issues and from whom every thing exists. He does not decree except with truth, and He does not act except with truth. Therefore, all things have a return to Him and a subsistence through Him; otherwise, they would be aimless and vain, and there is no aimlessness in creation, nor vanity in the making.

The evidence for His being characterized by these four attributes is that He, the Exalted, is Allah, the Necessary Existent (الموجود لذاته) who brings others into existence (الموجد لغيره).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ يَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ لا بُرْهانَ لَهُ بِهِ فَإِنَّما حِسابُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الْكافِرُونَ** — **Whoever invokes besides Allah another god of which he has no proof, his reckoning will indeed be with his Lord. Indeed the faithless will not be felicitous (117)**. What is meant by invoking another god "with Allah" is invoking it despite His existence, the Exalted, not necessarily invoking Him and another god together. For most or all of the polytheists (المشركين) do not invoke Allah, the Exalted, but rather invoke the partners they have established. It is also possible that "invocation" (الدعاء) means affirmation (الإثبات), for the affirmation of another god is inseparable from invoking him.

His saying: **لا بُرْهانَ لَهُ بِهِ** (of which he has no proof) is an explanatory qualification (قيد توضيحي) for "another god," as there is no other god for whom there could be a proof; rather, the proof stands for the absolute negation of any other god.

His saying: **فَإِنَّما حِسابُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ** (his reckoning will indeed be with his Lord) is a word of threat. It contains an exclusivity (قصر) of his reckoning to being with his Lord; no one else will interfere in what his reckoning necessitates of recompense—which is the Fire, as explicitly stated in the preceding verses—for it will inevitably strike him. Its ultimate meaning is the negation of intercessors (الشفعاء) and despair of the means of salvation. He completed this with His saying: "Indeed the faithless will not be felicitous."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقُلْ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ وَارْحَمْ وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ** — **Say, ‘My Lord, forgive and have mercy, and You are the best of those who are merciful’ (118)**. This is the conclusion of the Surah. In it, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is commanded to say what He narrated from His believing servants—that they say it in the world, and that the reward for that is success on the Day of Resurrection: "Indeed there was a part of My servants who used to say..." etc., verses 109 and 111 of the Surah.

By this, the discourse concludes with what it opened with at the beginning of the Surah: "Certainly the faithful have attained felicity" (قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ). The discussion regarding the meaning of the verse has already preceded.

## (Narrative Discourse)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "Whoever withholds a *qirat* of zakat (الزكاة) — *the mandatory religious tax on specific types of wealth* — is neither a believer nor a Muslim, and that is His saying, the Exalted: **رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ** (My Lord! Take me back that I may do righteous deeds in what I have left behind)."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through other paths from him عليه‌السلام and from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. What is meant is the application (انطباق) of the verse to the one who withholds zakat, not that it was revealed specifically regarding him.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَمِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** (and ahead of them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected), he said: "The intermediate realm (البرزخ) is a matter between two matters—and it is the reward and punishment between the world and the Hereafter. This is the saying of al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: 'By Allah, I do not fear for you except the intermediate realm—but when the matter comes to us, we are more entitled to you.'"

**I say:** The latter part was narrated in *al-Kafi* with its chain from 'Umar ibn Yazid from him عليه‌السلام.

In it, 'Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام said: "The grave is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise—or a pit from the pits of the Fire."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Abu Wallad al-Hannat from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said: I said to him: "May I be your ransom, they narrate—that the souls of the believers are in the crops of green birds around the Throne." He said: "No. The believer is more noble to Allah—than that He should place his soul in the crop of a bird—but rather in bodies like their bodies."

In it, with its chain from Abu Basir, Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: "The souls of the believers are in a tree in Paradise—eating of its food and drinking of its drink, and they say: 'Our Lord, establish the Hour for us, and fulfill for us what You promised us—and join the last of us with the first of us.'"

In it, also with its chain from Abu Basir from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said: "The souls are in the form of bodies in a tree in Paradise—recognizing one another and questioning one another—so when a soul arrives among the souls, they say: 'Leave it, for it has come from a great terror'—then they ask it: 'What did so-and-so do? And what did so-and-so do?' If it says to them: 'I left him alive,' they hope for him. And if it says to them: 'He has perished,' they say: 'He has fallen, he has fallen.'"

**I say:** The reports (أخبار) regarding the intermediate realm (البرزخ) and the details of what happens to the believers and others in it are numerous and reach the level of being widely transmitted (متواترة). A portion of them has already passed in various previous discussions.

In *Majma' al-Bayan*, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Every lineage (حسب) and kinship (نسب) is cut off on the Day of Resurrection except my lineage and my kinship."

**I say:** It seems the narration is from the path of the general group (الجماعة). It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several compilers of collections from al-Miswar ibn Makhramah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and its wording is: "Kinships (الأنساب) are cut off on the Day of Resurrection except my kinship, my connection (سببي), and my relationship by marriage (صهري)." And from several of them from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and its wording is: "Every connection and kinship is cut off on the Day of Resurrection except my connection and my kinship." And from Ibn 'Asakir from Ibn 'Umar from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and its wording is: "Every kinship and relationship by marriage is cut off on the Day of Resurrection except my kinship and my relationship by marriage."

In *al-Manaqib*, in the hadith of Tawus from Zayn al-'Abidin عليه‌السلام: "Allah created Paradise for the one who obeys and does good, even if he were an Abyssinian slave, and He created the Fire for the one who disobeys Him, even if he were a Qurayshite son—have you not heard the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَلا يَتَساءَلُونَ** (When the Trumpet is blown, there will be no ties of kinship between them on that day, nor will they ask of one another)? By Allah, nothing will benefit you tomorrow except a precedence you send forth of righteous deeds."

**I say:** The context of the verse seems to resist specification (التخصيص). Perhaps among the effects of his kinship صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is that his progeny are granted success for righteous deeds by which they benefit on the Day of Resurrection.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **تَلْفَحُ وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ** (The Fire will scorch their faces), he said: "It flames upon them and burns them; **وَهُمْ فِيها كالِحُونَ** (and they will be grim in it), meaning with open mouths and darkened faces."

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain from Abu Basir from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **رَبَّنا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنا شِقْوَتُنا** (Our Lord! Our wretchedness overcame us), he said: "By their deeds they became wretched."

In *al-'Ilal*, with its chain from Mas'adah ibn Ziyad, he said: A man said to Ja'far ibn Muhammad عليه‌السلام: "O Abu 'Abdillah, we were created for wonder." He said: "And what is that, for Allah's sake?" He said: "We were created for annihilation (الفناء)." He said: "Gently, O son of my brother! We were created for subsistence (البقاء)—and how can a Paradise that does not perish and a Fire that is not extinguished be annihilated? Rather, we only move from one abode to another."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ** (He will say, ‘How many...’) until His saying: **فَسْئَلِ الْعادِّينَ** (so ask those who keep count), he said: "Ask the angels who count the days against us, and write down our hours and our deeds that we acquired therein."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from Ayfa' ibn 'Abd al-Kala'i, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "When Allah admits the people of Paradise into Paradise and the people of Hell into Hell—He will say to the people of Paradise: 'How many years did you tarry on earth?' They will say: 'We tarried a day or part of a day.' He will say: 'How excellent was what you traded in a day or part of a day—My mercy, My pleasure, and My Paradise; dwell therein eternally, made to abide.'

Then He will say: 'O people of the Fire, how many years did you tarry on earth?' They will say: 'We tarried a day or part of a day.' So He will say: 'How evil was what you traded in a day or part of a day—My Fire and My wrath; stay therein eternally.'"

**I say:** There is some obscurity in the application of the meaning of the hadith to the verse given its context and what the similar verses testify to. The discussion regarding the indication of the verse, supported by evidence, has already preceded.
